current_fact.fact_text: On 11 April 2001, Australia’s men’s national football team set a staggering world record by beating American Samoa 31–0 in a World Cup qualifier. Forward Archie Thompson scored 13 goals in the match, a record for most goals by a player in a full international game.

episode.title: Sedna FM #05 - On The Go - Zero Zero
episode.description: An energetic mix for when you're on the move. Zero Zero captures that feeling of starting fresh, moving forward, and embracing the journey. Perfect for travel, walks, or just getting things done.
songs: Grace Jones - Pull up to the bumper
Easy star all star - Soul love
James Brown - Talking loud and saying nothing (remix)

current_fact.match_reason: A record-smashing football scoreline bursting with momentum fits an upbeat, on-the-move mix full of rhythmic drive and competitive energy.

queue.fact_text: On 11 April 2006, Iranian president Mahmoud Ahmadinejad announced that Iran had successfully enriched uranium for the first time, using cascades of centrifuges. The claim marked a turning point in the country’s nuclear program and intensified global debates over energy, science, and proliferation.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

queue.match_reason: A milestone in nuclear technology and its controversies parallels the rise of Auto-Tune: both are technical tools that sparked fierce debates over control, ethics, and how far innovation should go.

queue.fact_text: On 11 April 2012, a rare doublet of great earthquakes struck the Wharton Basin west of Sumatra, with the largest shock clocking a magnitude of 8.6. Unusually, they were huge strike‑slip quakes—more like horizontal tearing of the ocean floor than the typical thrust earthquakes that cause giant tsunamis.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

queue.match_reason: A powerful shift deep under the Indian Ocean connects with an episode about going below the surface and flowing like water, moving between calm and turbulence.

queue.fact_text: On 11 April 2023, jets of the Myanmar Air Force attacked the village of Pazigyi during a gathering, killing at least 100 civilians in one of the deadliest single airstrikes since the 2021 coup. The massacre highlighted how modern air power can be turned against the very population it claims to protect.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

queue.match_reason: A devastating event in Myanmar’s neighborhood is paired with a Southeast-Asia-rooted episode from Thailand, inviting reflection on the region, its people, and the contrast between everyday life and sudden violence.

queue.fact_text: On 11 April 2001, beyond his record goal haul that day, Australian striker Archie Thompson experienced a surreal football match where the scoreboard behaved more like a pinball machine than a serious qualifier. The lopsided 31–0 result later helped push FIFA to restructure qualifiers so tiny nations wouldn’t face such overwhelming mismatches.

episode.title: Morning Drops #51 - The Dancefloor
episode.description:
For International Women’s Day, this Morning Drop turns its focus to women on the dancefloor, not as a trend, but as a cultural force.

Across five tracks, Gaia follows a line from disco closers to hip hop club classics, tracing how the dancefloor became a place where women could take space, build confidence, and create connection, sometimes with friends, sometimes alone, always with music at the centre.

This episode isn’t about pointing fingers. It’s a gentle reminder that spaces like the dancefloor still matter: places where people can express themselves, meet each other with respect, and feel part of something bigger than a night out.

Tracklist:
Donna Summer - Last Dance (1978)
The Sequence  - Funk you up
Salt-N-Pepa - Push It
Queen Latifah - Ladies First
Alicia Bridges - I Love the Nightlife

queue.match_reason: A football match that turned into an almost carnival-like avalanche of goals aligns with a dancefloor-themed episode where joy, momentum, and sheer excess of movement define the experience.

queue.fact_text: On 11 April 1957’s path to Singaporean self-rule, local leaders began reimagining the island not just as a colonial port but as a future independent state with its own identity. This political shift helped set the stage for the multicultural, high-tech cityscape that now lights up satellite photos of Earth at night.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

queue.match_reason: A young, globally oriented city-state emerging and connecting to the world fits an episode about voyaging into new territories, rooted in diverse, border-crossing sounds.

queue.fact_text: On 11 April 1727, when Bach’s St Matthew Passion was first heard in Leipzig, many congregants weren’t prepared for its nearly three-hour scale and emotional force. It blurred the line between liturgy and musical drama, a kind of 18th‑century ‘concept album’ quietly expanding what sacred music could be.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: Bach’s boundary-stretching Passion as immersive narrative parallels trip‑hop’s cinematic, mood-driven albums that quietly redefined what a ‘song cycle’ could be centuries later.

queue.fact_text: On 11 April 2023 in Pazigyi, villagers had gathered for a local administrative opening when the airstrike hit, turning an everyday community moment into a scene of devastation in seconds. The gap between ordinary daily life and sudden catastrophe is part of what makes the event so haunting.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: An ordinary village day ruptured by violence is juxtaposed with an episode about fragile, awe-filled encounters with nature, emphasizing how human choices can either destroy or protect the everyday beauty of life on Earth.

published.fact_text: On 11 April 1727, Johann Sebastian Bach’s monumental St Matthew Passion was first performed at St. Thomas Church in Leipzig. Originally intended as modest church music, it later vanished from public performance for nearly a century before being rediscovered and hailed as one of the greatest works in Western music.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

published.match_reason: A deep, contemplative sacred work pairs with a meditative jazz episode that explores long-form, spiritual-feeling music.

published.fact_text: On 11 April 1809, during the Battle of the Basque Roads, the daring British captain Lord Cochrane led a night attack using fireships against a French fleet trapped in shallow waters. His audacious plan nearly destroyed the enemy, but a lack of support from his superior, Admiral Gambier, turned a potential total victory into a controversial, incomplete one.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A dramatic naval battle in treacherous shallows fits with an episode recorded literally at sea, full of wave-swaying, maritime energy.

published.fact_text: On 11 April 1814, the Treaty of Fontainebleau forced Napoleon Bonaparte to abdicate unconditionally and go into exile on the tiny island of Elba. Europe briefly exhaled, believing the Napoleonic storms to be over, unaware that the emperor’s dramatic return during the Hundred Days still lay ahead.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: Napoleon’s fall and forced exile mark the close of a turbulent era, echoing the episode’s theme of endings, storms passing, and cautious new beginnings.

published.fact_text: On 11 April 1856, during the Second Battle of Rivas in Nicaragua, 25‑year‑old drummer Juan Santamaría volunteered for a near-suicidal mission: he burned down the building sheltering American mercenary William Walker’s forces, turning the tide of the battle. He died in the attempt, but became Costa Rica’s most celebrated national hero.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

published.match_reason: A Latin American story of sacrifice and anti-imperialist struggle fits with an episode about Residente, whose work revolves around resistance, identity, and political courage.

published.fact_text: On 11 April 1908, the German armored cruiser SMS Blücher was launched as the last of her kind in the Imperial German Navy. Designed just before the world fully embraced the dreadnought battleship, she was already technically obsolete on the day she touched water.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

published.match_reason: A heavyweight warship at the transition between eras mirrors a mix that slowly evolves from heavy, atmospheric textures to a brisk, propulsive groove across worlds.

published.fact_text: On 11 April 1935, Britain, France, and Italy met on the shores of Lake Maggiore to form the Stresa Front, a short-lived alliance meant to oppose Nazi Germany’s rearmament. Within months, shifting interests and Mussolini’s invasion of Ethiopia would quietly unravel the pact.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

published.match_reason: An Italian lakeside summit of European powers connects with an Italian-anchored, mountain-themed episode that weaves together cross-border European sounds.

published.fact_text: On 11 April 1945, American forces liberated the Buchenwald concentration camp near Weimar, Germany, finding over 20,000 survivors and harrowing evidence of systematic brutality. The camp’s own clandestine resistance committee had already seized control of parts of the site just before the troops arrived.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: Such a grave historical moment calls for a reflective, gentle soundtrack; this slow, downtempo episode offers space for remembrance and quiet contemplation.

published.fact_text: On 11 April 1957, the United Kingdom agreed to grant Singapore internal self-government, setting the city-state on a path from colonial trading outpost to one of the most globally connected hubs on Earth. Within less than a decade, it would become a fully independent country.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

published.match_reason: A story of a small maritime territory growing into its own ties well with an episode themed around seafaring journeys and the music of trading coasts and harbors.

published.fact_text: On 11 April 1964, Marshal Humberto de Alencar Castelo Branco was elected President of Brazil by Congress, cementing the control of the military regime that had just seized power in a coup. His technocratic rule helped shape decades of authoritarian governance across South America.

episode.title: Morning Drops #46 - Fog and Fire
episode.description: In today’s Morning Drops, Gaia brings us on a passport-free trip between two Italian energies: the foggy stillness of the North and the volcanic pulse of the South. Throughout the journey, Gaia shares four of her favourite Italian songwriters. Grab your coffee and tune into the radio show from another planet!
songs: Edoardo Bennato - La Torre Di Babele
Lucio Dalla - Com'è Profondo il Mare

published.match_reason: The tension between order and unrest in a military regime resonates with an episode built around contrasts—fog and fire—as expressed through politically aware Italian songwriters.

published.fact_text: On 11 April 1965, a catastrophic Palm Sunday tornado outbreak unleashed 55 tornadoes across six Midwestern U.S. states in just a few hours. The storms carved parallel scars across the landscape, killing 266 people and reshaping how Americans thought about severe weather forecasting.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: A violent outbreak of atmospheric electricity matches the episode’s theme of electric energy and high-voltage guitar storms.

published.fact_text: On 11 April 1970, NASA launched Apollo 13, a lunar mission that would become famous not for landing on the Moon, but for surviving a catastrophic onboard explosion. The crew’s improvised repairs and the ground team’s ingenuity turned a potential tragedy into one of spaceflight’s most dramatic rescues.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A near-fatal space mission that became a lesson in creative problem-solving aligns perfectly with an episode about reimagining ‘empty’ space as a canvas for possibility.

published.fact_text: On 11 April 1981, Brixton in south London erupted in one of the most significant urban riots in modern British history, triggered by tensions over racist policing and economic hardship. For three days, burning cars and street battles forced the country to confront structural inequalities many preferred not to see.

episode.title: Morning Drops #17 - Steel City
episode.description: Gaia takes us on a short trip through Sheffield's sounds — from electronic textures to raw guitars and sharp storytelling. Four tracks, one city, different edges of the same story.
songs: The Human League - The Sound Of The Crowd

published.match_reason: A story about a British city defined by class and social tensions parallels an episode centered on another UK city, Sheffield, whose bands narrate everyday urban struggles and working-class life.

published.fact_text: On 11 April 1987, Israel’s Shimon Peres and Jordan’s King Hussein secretly signed the London Agreement, sketching out a framework for Arab–Israeli peace negotiations that never fully materialized. The document foreshadowed later accords but also revealed how fragile back-channel diplomacy can be.

episode.title: Morning Drops #32 - A Weekend In London
episode.description: Recorded in London, marking a weekend reunion between Gaia and Yasmin. A selection inspired by London's energy, seen through the eyes of two inseparable friends — music from concerts, club nights, and long walks across town. Four tracks to ease you into the week at the right pace.
songs: Jimmy Castor Bunch - It's Just Begun
The Asphodells - Late Flowering Lust (Phil Kieran Remix)

published.match_reason: A clandestine peace framework signed in London matches an episode grounded in London’s streets and rhythms, hinting at the city’s role as a quiet stage for world-changing conversations.

published.fact_text: On 11 April 1990, customs officers in Middlesbrough, England, seized a massive steel tube being shipped to Iraq that turned out to be part of "Project Babylon"—Saddam Hussein’s attempt to build a gigantic supergun capable of firing shells hundreds of kilometers. The project was eventually dismantled after a trail of suspicious shipments and assassinations.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: A secret project turning engineering into a weapon contrasts with an episode about technology’s impact on our lives, both asking how far we let tools shape our world and our behavior.

published.fact_text: On 11 April 1993, anti-fascist youth activist Guillem Agulló was stabbed to death by neo-Nazis while camping in Montanejos, Spain. His murder galvanized a generation of Catalan and Valencian activists, turning his name into a symbol chanted at protests decades later.

episode.title: Morning Drops #08 - (un)Boxing
episode.description: An episode about breaking free from expectations and labels. Music that helps us unbox ourselves and embrace who we truly are.
songs: Kendrick Lamar - The Blacker the Berry

published.match_reason: A young man killed for his beliefs and identity pairs with an episode about refusing boxes and labels, supported by politically sharp, identity-centered Kendrick Lamar tracks.

published.fact_text: On 11 April 1993, 450 inmates at the Southern Ohio Correctional Facility in Lucasville began a prison uprising that would last ten days, leaving nine inmates and a guard dead. Among their grievances was the forced tuberculosis vaccination of Nation of Islam prisoners, raising complex questions about religion, health, and state power.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A prison revolt over rights and state control is well matched with an episode dedicated to Rage Against the Machine, whose music centers on systemic injustice and resistance.

published.fact_text: On 11 April 2001, Australia’s men’s national football team set a staggering world record by beating American Samoa 31–0 in a World Cup qualifier. Forward Archie Thompson scored 13 goals in the match, a record for most goals by a player in a full international game.
